title: Is Awareness Enough?
subtitle: The Role of Intelligence in the Process of Enlightenment
paragraphs: I thought I'd try to put things together here, to make clear what role awareness plays in the process of enlightenment — and what else is necessary. If we begin with the life of the Buddha himself, remember that he had practised quite fiercely for six years. And before that, he probably dabbled a bit.

In the first year or so, he attains all these very profound absorptions — what we now call the jhānas — to the point that both his teachers ask him to join them as a teacher. During that time he's obviously keeping his mind very still, because one of the factors of the jhānas is a still mind, a concentrated mind.

Then he does mortification exercises, mainly around not eating. And he comes to the end of this process after six years and he's got nowhere. Nowhere in terms of his koan, his real life question: is there an end to suffering? Now all that time, he had awareness.

He comes to that point, and in desperation he's sitting by the roadside looking pretty ragged. A woman comes along with a bowl of rice, meaning to offer it to a god. In the scriptures they say she mistook him for a deva, for an angel, for a god — but I don't think he looked like that at all. I think she took pity on him and said, you'd better take some of this rice.
He had this rice, and he felt fulfilled. He must have been pretty miserable. His mates had left him — they said he'd gone soft because he'd eaten his rice.

And then he remembered a little time in childhood, when he was observing his father doing a ploughing ceremony. There was something in that observation, something in that particular moment, which he remembered now, in a time of desperation, looking back over his life, wondering whether there was an end to suffering. He remembers this particular state of mind.

If you go back, can you remember a particular state of mind at the age of eight, nine, ten, twelve? It must have been something he really remembered — something about the quality of that observation. And of course, he then goes and sits and determines not to move until his eyes are enlightened or he drops dead.
Have you tried that? He sat there, and luckily for him — and for us — he became enlightened. He had this insight which liberated him from the confusion about suffering. That's his story.

Then, of course, the whole of his life is actually talking about this moment, about how to get to this moment of liberation. And the core discourse is the Satipaṭṭhāna discourse — the discourse on how to establish this sati, which we translate as mindfulness, awareness, attention, being present. All these are about being here in a particular way.

If we look at this discourse, you'll see the words he uses. He says that the meditator sits and observes four frames of reference: the body, feelings, the mind with its emotions, and dhamma — by which he means looking at all this from the point of view of his teachings. The three characteristics, the four noble truths, eight of these, ten of those, and so on.

And he says to do it with ātāpī, sampajāno, satimā. Now ātāpī comes from the word tapas, which means mortification exercise. In other words, it's real energy. Those people who translate the Zen 'effortless effort' as meaning you can just lie down and it'll happen haven't read this particular discourse. There's some energy here. We need some energy. We need some commitment. We need some real putting of our effort behind something.

The word satimā is just an adjective from sati — mindfully, with awareness. And the middle word is sampajāno, meaning with intelligence. These core words are in constant recurrence throughout the discourse.

When we get into the discourse itself, which describes in more detail how we establish these four frames of reference — the body, feelings in the body, emotional states, mental states, and the Dhamma — we find these recurring phrases. The Buddha says to establish mindfulness on one of these four, and to do it inwardly just to the point of being able to stay with it. Then the next stage is to observe the rising and falling. And finally he says to dwell upon the object of meditation just so much that ñāṇa and paṭisati can arise naturally. Ñāṇa is insight; sati is awareness. Nowhere in the scripture are sati and paññā separated. Wherever there's sati, there's paññā.

If we go back to sati, go back to awareness — a robber, in the midst of robbing a bank, is ultra-aware, isn't he? I'd be. Very mindful. My head would be sparking. When you're crossing a busy road and you've got to watch yourself, you're aware. But there's something about the awareness that leads to enlightenment which is not ordinary awareness. That's why the Buddha calls it sammā-sati — right awareness.
A baby is aware. In fact, if anything, a child lives almost entirely in the present moment, but it's not sammā-sati. There must be something else to the sati.

If we turn now to the factors of enlightenment, we come across those qualities which must also be present against this background of sati. Sati, you can say, is like a field of awareness — just a being present. But something must come into that presence which is going to spark, which is actually going to see something.

What we find in the seven factors is this dhamma-vicaya, which means investigation of dhamma. Although we have this awareness, it has to be pointed at something. It has to be aware of something. There has to be an intentionality behind it. You can't just flop into this wondrous, spacious state of being all things, a state of open awareness, because nothing's going to happen. It's very nice, very peaceful, very beautiful — but it's dull, it's daft. Nothing happens.

There has to come in this dhamma-vicaya, this investigation of Dhamma, and it has to be balanced with upekkhā — equanimity. This suggests that the investigation must not have a question there. That's the problem. As soon as we say, let's investigate something, a question arises. Somehow we have to develop that curiosity which is just a question mark. As soon as there's a word put to it, as soon as there's a looking for something, then it's going to be corrupted — corrupted by the concept you're looking for.

Now balanced with that you have pīti and passaddhi. Pīti here translates as interest. Interest is an attitude, isn't it? It's a mental state to have interest in something. There's a sort of eagerness included in there, a wanting to know. It's a heart state to be interested in something.

That has to be balanced with passaddhi, which is tranquillity. If interest isn't balanced that way, it becomes excited. And if there's no interest there, then you simply enter into this lovely meditative state of tranquillity.

You can see meditators when they do that. They'll be like this. And if you wake them up, they say, 'Oh, I'm here. What's wrong? I'm aware.' Put them to sleep. It's lovely. Nothing's going to happen. They're going to end up after an hour just as daft as they were before they started.

There has to be this intelligence, this interest, this wanting to know. And subsisting all that, supporting all that, is of course concentration — this one-pointedness of the mind, the ability of the mind to stay still. And the effort — this is the ātāpī, the effort of mortification exercises — is just enough effort to maintain that. That's all.

When we meditate and sit here, the first effort is to bring the mind to stillness. But once you're in that stillness, once you've made the effort to quiet down and become aware of the object, there has to arise in us this wanting to know.

Now, what is it we want to know? What does that wanting have to be pointed to? Here, the Buddha simply says to become aware of the three characteristics of existence. Because when we become aware of the three characteristics, we're undermining this consciousness which believes itself to be a self.

As long as we are aware of transience — and that's his next step, you see. The first step was simply to dwell upon the object. The second step is to become aware of its process. But to my mind, this watching of anicca, this watching of transiency, includes the other two: observing how suffering arises, and observing that quality of not me, not mine. There are your three characteristics.

Now, you can see that this intuitive intelligence, which is natural to us, is the same intelligence that works out how much we have to pay for the bills and what we have to do at work. It's all the same intelligence — there's no other intelligence than the intelligence we have. What we need to do is clarify it: clarify the intelligence of thought, and clarify the intelligence of any distorting emotion.

We do that by making states of mind into objects. We do that by bringing the mind into the present moment so that thought can't get in — because thought is always after thought. When you're involved in something, there's no room for thought.

The whole process of meditation is to close down the awareness, as it were, into the present moment — to an object arising and passing away — so that there's no time but to see what there is. But that seeing is guided. It's actually being primed to see the three factors, the three characteristics of existence.

The fact that it's arising and passing away. The fact that it's not me, not mine — that distance. And the fact that any time I form a relationship with it, of wanting or not wanting, possession or identity, some dissatisfaction arises.

It's just watching this that liberates consciousness from wrong relationship to the body, mind, and heart it's in. That's all we're doing — changing our relationship to the world. And remember, in Buddhist terms the world is how we experience the world. In other words, it's within this, as the Buddha says, fathom-long body. It's not outside us. It's within this bubble of our consciousness. That's what we've got to change.

Now, considering the Buddha's own discovery of the role of awareness — when you think about it, it can't only have been awareness. It must have also been the memory of this intuition that came with it, this way of looking.

The example I always use is of a little child who's around about pre-language, or just at the beginning of language, where there's no conceptual thinking as such. They might have a word like 'car' or whatever, but there's nothing built up around it conceptually.

You'll often see them spot something like a bug they've never seen before. And you see them just lock onto it. There's no time as far as they're concerned — just this thing crawling away. You'll see their mouths drop. Now, when the mouth drops and the tongue relaxes, there's no thought. There they are, just in that moment, stuck there. And then some well-meaning parent will tell them to shut their mouth and kill it.

But if you leave the child alone, of course it comes out of it and says, 'What is it?' Once the parent says, 'Oh, that's a beetle,' that's it — the child doesn't look anymore. It knows it. And we've lost that direct contact with beetles. When was the last time you really looked at a beetle?

That's what concepts do. They make us presume that in seeing a thing through the prism of a concept, we actually experience it. Somehow we've got to go beyond that, and we do it by constantly bringing attention into the present moment. As it locks into the present moment, there can't be room for thought. And we're right there with that childlike mind.

Except this time, it actually has some intelligence behind it, some stored wisdom, which the child doesn't have. If the child had that, it'd become enlightened at the age of three. But we have to go through the horror of our lives in order to build up the wisdom of how to develop this way of looking. Remember, it's suffering which leads us to enlightenment. We've got to go through the pain, or else there's no gain. Everybody knows that.

This brings us to a common confusion about the different traditions. As far as my understanding goes — and you're welcome to correct me after the talk — there are two major approaches. There's the Vipassana tradition, and there's the Dzogchen-Zazen tradition. And just on the outside of those, there's the Koan tradition.

In the Vipassana process, we want to get to that sati-paññā, that sampajāno satimā. And we do it by using an object within the body — the breath itself. By turning inward and going into the breath, we pull out that singular object and begin to bury our awareness and intuitive intelligence into a single sensation. All the world is in a grain of sand.

Then comes the moment when we open up to the field of awareness, so that we're not just concentrated into the breath but able to observe anything that draws our attention. We're not looking for anything. I'm sitting watching the breath and there's a bird song. It's the bird song that draws my attention — I'm not looking for bird songs.

As I go to the ear, to the ear base, I'm interested in the process of hearing. I'm not interested in the bird. I'll see the image of the bird in my mind. I hear and I feel my response to it. So I can see this whole connection I have with the outside world. I can see that when the bird stops, there's a longing for it to go on a little bit more. There's my attachment. That's what I'm interested in — the way this mind is constantly wandering between wanting and not wanting, how things arise and pass away. Creating that distance, recognising it's not me, not mine. And it's all come from this centre in the breath where I've honed the seven factors of enlightenment.

Now, if you go to the Dzogchen school or the Zazen school — the Zazen school I know personally — as soon as you sit, you're supposed to be there. Like that. Boom, boom. You made it. That's it. You're right there, just where it took these poor Vipassana ninnies weeks to get. These Dzogchen guys — boom, that's it, they're there.

When you look at their training, you can see how they get there and are able to hold it. Remember that in most cases — like with someone like Sogyal Rinpoche, as far as I know — you sit in Dzogchen for ten minutes. That's all they can expect from somebody doing their practice at an ordinary level.
Remember that insight in the Enlightenment doesn't take any time. You either see it or you don't. It's a flash — that's it, it's finished.

But if we look at the whole tradition, we see how they can just sit there for ten minutes, or maybe an hour, or whatever. A hundred thousand bows. Om Mani Padme Hum, days on end. What are those practices? Visualisations, purifying the heart. It's only when you've been through all that can you throw yourself on the cushion and enter into Dzogchen. There's the balance of the practice.

Dzogchen masters and Zazen masters will tell you that the error in their traditions is the same error Vipassana people fall into: moving into that state of clear awareness, body absolutely straight, perfect energy — but no intelligence. Perfect peace and happiness until they wake up.

If you look at the Zazen school, you've only got to go up to Throssel Hole. It's been years since I went there, but when I used to go, you'd work three hours at a stretch. You'd never stop. And you worked fast.

I remember once the abbot wanted soil moved from one end of the grounds to the other to build a little garden. So I was stood at one end with a spade, another guy stood at the other end, and there was this Dominican priest with a wheelbarrow, belting up and down. He shoved it in and down, chucked it out and back up. Three hours! Weeding for three hours.

The whole idea of Zazen meditation is that it's not something separate from what you do. It's a common mistake, especially in Vipassana, to treat these as special practices for special occasions. But in Zen they always talk about ordinary daily life.

So there you are weeding, and you're putting your mind in it. You've heard these Zen sayings: when you wash the pots, just wash the pots. When you weed, you weed. You get into the rhythm of it, and you don't want to leave. The bell goes, and you want to keep weeding. Then you go back inside, full of this energy, and boom — something's going to go, and you're there.

The koan is interesting, because it actually has its roots — all these things have their roots — in the fundamental schools of Theravada and, for the Mahayana, what became known as the Sarvāstivādin school.

If you have a look at this very dry tome called the Path of Purification by Buddhaghosa, a scholar of the medieval ages — the driest book in the world, a bag of bones — it's probably the most brilliant compendium of spiritual exercises you'll come across. And one of them is how to become enlightened by repeating the word death.

How to become enlightened by repeating the word death. How are we supposed to do that? Well, the idea is that you sit, you be silent, and you use the word death to contact your feelings about death. Every time you say it, images may come, feelings may come. It's an internalised practice of what's in the Satipaṭṭhāna Sutta, known as looking at dead bodies in cemeteries — the asubha, the horrible meditations.

You're supposed to go to a cemetery and a charnel ground where bodies were just left and eaten by vultures, rotting, rats all around. You sit there and watch bodies decomposing. You come back, see them in different states, and keep saying to yourself: just as this body is, so mine will be. The commentary warns you not to sit windward. Very detailed.

So there you are sitting, repeating this word death, and it's bringing up all your emotions about it, all your feelings. Just like in Vipassana, you observe in part one. And who is it that's afraid of death? What is it? It's the self, isn't it? Once you've cut through the fear of death, what is there to be afraid of? Hence the enlightenment.
From that you can see how the koans came. The regular koan in somewhere like Korea is who am I or what is it.

In the old days, a person would have been given a koan, and they would have been caught up in finding an intellectual answer. The Rinzai school was meant for samurais and intellectuals. So the yogi would come along, and the master would ask him the koan and wait for the answer. The guy would give him some sort of explanation — well, I'm not this, I'm not that — and he'd get a clout across the ear. He'd keep coming, getting bonked on the head, until finally he stopped thinking. That's the point of the koan: to stop thinking.

So the idea in the koan, when we say something like who am I — and remember, these koans come up naturally in your meditation. You're just feeling a feeling and you hear a little question: what is this? That's your koan.

It's turning on that intelligence. That's what the koan is doing — making that intelligence look in a certain way, but beyond thought. So the meditator, just like one would say death and feel what comes up, might say who am I, and just rest in that moment and let the intelligence take over.
Our problem is we keep getting in the way. We keep trying to manipulate that intelligence to see what we want it to see and become enlightened.
So you can see how the koan is just an extension, a more obvious way of exciting this paññā, this intuitive intelligence we have within us.

Now you can see where the confusion arises. If you come from the Zen tradition and meet a Theravadin who says, 'All you need is awareness,' you might think, 'Oh no, no, no — that won't do at all.' But in the Theravada understanding, whenever we say right awareness, the intelligence is included. It's not right awareness unless the intuitive intelligence is being activated. That's where the confusion often lies when these schools meet — they're coming from slightly different traditions.

But it's always the same. Always the same. In his book The Heart of Buddhist Meditation, still considered a classic, Nyanaponika Thera — a very famous German monk who died not so long ago — noted that there was a time when people thought Theravada meditation and Zen were opposites, belonging to two different worlds. But when these teachers came together, when people who had practised both began to compare notes, they realised it's all one and the same thing. And when you think about it, how else could it be?

So there we have it. The Buddha's own remembrance in childhood of a particular level of consciousness. It's not a state of mind. It's not an emotional state. It's not a thought state. It's a way of seeing things.

Because of his own practice, he has that concentration of mind. He has the effort. And he has the rice pudding. He sits under the tree and becomes enlightened. And then he realises what the factors were that got him there.

The main factor is this sati. Behind it are all the faculties. Why does he put sati first? Because that's your main disposition — awareness. To begin with, awareness of the object is your disposition. Once the awareness is there, concentration and effort will rise to support it.

Once awareness and concentration are there, coming more and more into the present moment, the intuitive wisdom — supported by faith, because if there's any scepticism you've had it — uses those qualities to see clearly what's going on. And just that seeing is its own liberation.
The liberation is consciousness liberating itself from wrong view. And the whole process of enlightenment is simply to get out of the way. The effortless effort. That's it.
Are there any questions? Observations? Criticism? Additions?

There are many stories about people who follow teachers and are then severely treated in order to get rid of this ego — which you mentioned is one of the biggest barriers to having these states come together in the right proportions and go forward. How does that strike you? Do you go out scourging yourself on weekends?
I don't think Buddhist teachers were particularly cruel. The only real stories come from the Zen tradition, where they chop your finger off or something.

Well, remember that the way teachers teach their students depends very much on the culture. I can't imagine a Westerner taking that sort of business — they'd probably smack the person back. It's not within our culture to be battered towards enlightenment. The Buddha himself wasn't. Nobody gave him a whack or two.

As Buddhism spreads, remember, it picks up the culture. In the West we're in very early days, and I can well imagine that within three or four generations, Buddhism in the West will be remarkably different from Buddhism elsewhere. Because that's what's happened everywhere else. Chinese Buddhism is remarkably different from Japanese, from Thai, from Tibetan. It changes.

But your core question is really about how to get rid of ego and the self. One of the things that's very difficult for us is the word obedience — the word submission. We can't handle that. It goes against the whole post-modern idea that I do my own thing, in my own way, in my own time.

Before the Buddha became enlightened, so it is said, he put his bowl into the river and it flowed upwards against the stream. Only dead fish go with the flow. This was symbolic of the fact that whatever we do towards enlightenment is always going to go against the self. Always.

The self, remember, is caught up in a game — a game with the world of wanting and not wanting. And it finds a certain pleasure there, a certain comfort. It doesn't want to let go because it always thinks that if it can just get things right, it will definitely be perfectly happy.

That's one of the things about bowing. People hate to bow. But bowing is the bodily expression of that submission — the physical expression of submitting yourself to the diktats of the Dhamma. So if the Dhamma says you shouldn't indulge in this, then you have to bow to that. If you can't bow to it, if you can't take the teaching, if you can't do it, then you just end up with a very stiff back. An arthritic back.

This is one of the fundamental attitudes of any spiritual life — not just Buddhism. Humility goes all the way through. This word humility, which has now been downgraded into slavery. But humility is that ability to be obedient. Who uses the word obedient these days? You don't hear parents use it with kids anymore. It's all gone out of the language. Too painful for the ego.

The word discipline — it actually means, if I remember rightly, study. Disciplina. It means learning. So in a university you have a discipline. We talk about a discipline because to study, to advance in knowledge, there has to be this commitment, this order. But these days, education is much more concerned with entertainment.

So, in answer to your question about the way teachers teach, it's often to humble the student. I remember when I was in Burma, being the Westerner, I just got fed up of bowing to this monk. He was my teacher, but I got fed up with him. He was so conceited, as far as I could see. Arrogant. I wasn't going to bow to him.

I remember approaching him and just sitting there. And he went... And if I hadn't bowed, I'd have been out. That's it. Forget it. What he was teaching me is: look, if you're going to take my message, then you'd better have the right attitude. One of the most painful times of my life.
Is the awareness with interest and energy — what exactly do you mean by energy?

Energy is just the effort to maintain it. As a physical thing, the only energy you need to become enlightened — if we could hold it there, the Buddha says for seven years if you're unlucky, for seven days if you're really with it — it's just the energy to keep you upright. Just the energy in the spine.

All the energy you need is the resolution, the energy to just keep observing the object. That's why I mentioned to those of you here for the meditation that if we are serial meditators, then every time we come to sit, we really have to remind ourselves what we're going to do. So we make that resolution: this period of time, I'm going to commit myself to establishing mindfulness. Put it in your own way.

Unless you forget, and you just sit there, it becomes habitual — the mind wanders, and sure, you've planned your holiday down to America, but what's the point? If you sit and make this resolution right at the beginning of your sit, then I think you'll find that when you wander, you'll come back to that same resolution. Because remember, that resolution is conditioning your mind, conditioning you to be present. It's an attitudinal change.

And I would say that every time you lose it on some dream aside, you come back, you stop for a moment, you recognise what's happened. And you recommit yourself with real resolution. Resolute resolution.
But don't you need a free flow of energy, without tension?

The thing is, once you're in this posture and you're lifting the energy up the spine just by sitting tall, you're actually correcting all the energy in your body. You will feel — I'm sure many of you have felt — all the chakras fire. Pain in your stomach, pain in your chest, around your neck: it's all blocked energy. That's it. You don't have to do any yoga posture to become enlightened. You don't need astrology. Sorry.

You seem to be saying that people can have quite deep insights into those three experiences, but not have a final cessation of suffering. Maybe some change in ways that can happen, bringing wisdom and intelligence, but there's still a sufferer and suffering can still take place.

Yes. The insights into these things are like little corrections of view. Little corrections, until the whole thing becomes properly corrected and then you're enlightened. They're just little adjustments. And they show up in little changes in the way you behave, in the way you react to similar situations, in the way you don't react to similar situations. Little changes within you. It's a very slow process. The Buddha often warns us this is a slow process.
And then there are kernel points when we can talk about a shift in perception. These are what we call the stream-entrant, the non-returner, and the arahat.

I liken it to a stick. You keep bending the stick. Now you'll notice that when you first start bending a stick, it's easy, isn't it? So when you first sit, it's easy. But as you keep going, you get the resistance. And that's the ego pulling away. You've just got to keep working. Sometimes you relax and back up again. But remember that every time you flex it, you are getting stronger. You all do these exercises, I'm sure. And then one day — crack! Two sticks. Different state. That's how it goes.
My question was going to be: once the stick is broken, is there no going back?

Definitely not. And who'd want to go back? Think of the shift of consciousness, this very great shift that we go through between six and eight, where we get real. When we're six, believing Father Christmas comes down the chimney is not a problem, because what I can imagine is true — it's fact. Eight is a bit difficult. And then this morality comes in. It's not as though young children don't feel shame and guilt, but it becomes much more clear around the age of seven: right and wrong. The tree of the fruit of good and evil. Now you wouldn't want to go back to three years old. I don't think so. So once you've moved, once you've become a sotāpanna, a stream-entrant, or an arahat, you don't want to go down again.
Is enlightenment one thing from which there is no going back, or is it also a temporary insight?

Actually, it's a good question because this is very confusing across the different traditions. In Theravāda we have things like jhānas, these insights of vipassanā. In the Japanese tradition we hear about satori and kenshō. And they're actually all the same thing. When you read the literature and go through all the traditions, they're always talking about the same thing — little insights concerning the three facts, the three characteristics. Transience, how suffering arises, and not-self. It's always that.

And then you'll see that they often have a special word for what we would call stream-entrance, and so on. Now, up until that stick snaps, you can always go back. And it's very difficult. It really is. Oh, it's soul-destroying to have got the stick there and to have given up. It's like climbing up the mountain — you're almost at the peak and you tumble all the way down.

So it's very important not to push ourselves so hard that we exhaust ourselves. We have to take a pace that's our personal pace of enlightenment. Just our own pace. Some people walk round and round, spiralling up the mountain, and the occasional person can go straight up. We've all got our little way.
And the other mistake, of course, is to overestimate — that's the other side, where you think you've made it. That can be very depressing when you find out you haven't.

I've had two experiences, just personal experiences, where it was really just a pain. I came to a point where I thought, God, no, I can't handle this. I was just going to cut out and get back to a bit of happiness, for God's sake. It was one of those little moments in your spiritual life. And I actually made a decision to give up.

All I can say is that the next moment I was filled with an intense horror at what that meant. The horror was so awful within me that I just went and sat on the cushion and waited until the intention to meditate came again.

I've also had the experience of thinking you've achieved something when actually you haven't. That's a bit depressing. It takes a couple of beers to get over that. And then you gather energy and start off again.

The ego gets more and more subtle the more you chase it. The more you overcome ego, the more subtle it becomes. Remember, conceit stays with you to the bitter end — it's one of the last fetters to go. Restlessness is one of the last fetters too. And restlessness here suggests every neurosis you can think of, but extraordinarily refined. It's still there. The fear's still there, the anger's still there. Everything's still there, right to the bitter end.
It's a little more fun going at your own pace. But how do you know if you're being lazy, not trying hard at all, or overdoing it?

Well, I think it's a case of personal experiment. In terms of laziness — I like to use my own definition really — laziness is when you decide you're not going to do it. When you decide that instead you can have a cup of tea. It's actually a conscious decision: I'm fed up.

Sloth and torpor are slightly more subtle because they're mental states that arise. My teacher used to call them our two very good friends, because they're always saying, 'Ten minutes won't do you any harm. Just have a kip. You'll feel so good.' But in fact, it's not tiredness at all. It's just sloth and torpor.

So in a sense you've got to experiment. You've got to experiment and just be very aware of your body, very aware of where the limits are. When it came to sloth and torpor, and just general laziness and not wanting to do things, this was the great problem of Moggallāna, the second great disciple of the Buddha. The Buddha gave him, if I remember rightly, thirteen different ways to awaken, to stay above the sloth and torpor. Only at the end of all that could he say he was really tired and have a sleep.

It's like you're trying to push the boundary, but not so much that you crack. And you have to be prepared to make mistakes. You don't know the limit until you make a mistake. But if you go warily, the mistake doesn't crush you.

I'm just going back now to the seventies, the middle seventies, where so many people I knew really got into this business of these insights — which are all delineated — and just exhausted themselves with this very wrong effort of trying to achieve something. One very good friend of mine was in Burma, actually. He dropped out. I could see it happening. I met him about seven years later, and that year he'd spent ten thousand pounds on crack. So you can fall a long way.
I'm not telling you this to frighten you — I'm not suggesting you're going to rush out and develop a crack habit. But it is a matter of experimentation, of working within your limits.
